content.text: Tool-awareness prompting teaches the model which external functions are available, what they do, and when to invoke them. The quality of the tool description directly controls whether the model calls the right tool, passes the right arguments, and knows when not to call any tool at all. A well-described tool is one the model can use correctly on the first call; a poorly described tool leads to wrong arguments, unnecessary calls, or the model hallucinating a tool that does not exist.
Anatomy of a Good Tool Description
Field
Purpose
Common mistake
name
Unique identifier the model uses when invoking the tool
Ambiguous names (get_data, run) that do not signal intent
description
When to use this tool; what it returns; what it does NOT do
Only describing what it does, not when to use it or its limits
parameters
JSON Schema defining each argument: type, description, constraints, required vs optional
Missing descriptions on parameters; no enum for constrained values
return description
What the tool's response looks like; what fields to read
Omitting return shape; model guesses how to interpret the response
Defining Tools with the Anthropic SDK
Description Quality: Weak vs Strong
Weak description
Problem
Strong description
"Gets data from the database."
What data? When to use it?
"Returns a user's order history by user ID. Use when the user asks about past purchases. Does not return payment details."
"Search tool for finding things."
Model cannot distinguish it from other search tools
"Searches the product catalogue only. Use when the user asks about availability, specs, or pricing of products. Does not search support docs or orders."
"Sends an email."
No scope guard; model may call it too readily
"Sends a transactional email to the authenticated user only. ONLY call this after explicit user confirmation. Cannot send to third parties."

The description field does most of the work. It should answer three questions: what does the tool return, when should the model call it (positive trigger), and when should the model NOT call it (negative trigger or scope limit). Positive triggers prevent missed calls; negative triggers prevent spurious calls. Both are necessary for reliable tool use — a tool described only by what it does, without when and when-not, leaves the model to guess the boundaries.
Why Tool Descriptions Matter

When you call the Anthropic API with a tools array, the API serialises your tool definitions into the model's context window as part of the system prompt (you can observe this with the anthropic-beta: token-counting header). The model reads the description field exactly like it reads any other text in its context — there is no special semantic processing. The description is the model's only source of knowledge about what the tool does, when to call it, and what its output looks like.

A vague description like "Gets data." forces the model to guess the tool's purpose from context clues in the conversation. If the user asks for "recent orders", the model may call a payment_info tool because the description didn't clarify the scope. A precise description like "Returns the last 30 days of order history for the authenticated user. Does not return payment method or billing address." leaves no room for misinterpretation.
The description also controls
negative triggers

— situations where the model should choose NOT to call the tool. Without explicit negative triggers, the model may call send_email whenever a user mentions email, even if they are just asking what an email is. Phrases like "Only call after the user explicitly confirms the action" or "Do NOT call if the user is asking a general question" are negative triggers that significantly reduce spurious invocations.
Tool Description Architecture

attrs.alt: Tool description → LLM decision flow: tool definitions + user message feed the LLM, which checks description for match, then either emits a tool_use block (dispatched to your function) or skips/hallucinates. Good descriptions answer 5 questions about the tool.

content.text: Benefits of High-Quality Tool Descriptions
Correct tool selection.
The model picks the right tool for the task on the first attempt, reducing multi-turn correction overhead.
Accurate argument construction.
Detailed parameter descriptions (with enums, ranges, and examples) cause the model to pass valid arguments, reducing dispatch errors.
Reduced hallucinated calls.
Negative triggers ("Do NOT call if...") prevent the model from invoking tools when they are not applicable.
Self-documenting system.
Good descriptions serve as the API documentation for the LLM layer — developers reading the tool definition understand both the function's purpose and the model's intended use.
Fewer prompt engineering iterations.
Well-described tools require less system prompt patching to correct tool selection behaviour.
Better handling of ambiguous requests.
Explicit scope limits help the model correctly decline to use a tool when the user's intent does not match the tool's purpose.
Trade-offs
Verbose descriptions consume tokens.
Each description is part of the input to every API call. 5 tools with 200-word descriptions each add ~1,000 tokens to every request. At scale this adds up.
Description drift.
When the underlying function changes (new parameters, changed return format) but the description is not updated, the model's behaviour diverges from the tool's actual capabilities.
Conflicting descriptions.
Two tools with overlapping descriptions cause the model to call either one depending on minor wording in the user message — unpredictable selection.
Language-model dependence.
Different models respond differently to the same description. A description tuned for Claude may need adjustment for GPT-4 or Llama.
When to Use Structured Tool Descriptions vs System Prompt Instructions
Concern
Tool description field
System prompt
Reason
What the tool returns
Yes
Sometimes
Goes in description: close to where model decides to call it
When to call the tool
Yes — positive trigger
Optionally reinforce
Primary: in description. Reinforce in system prompt for critical tools
When NOT to call the tool
Yes — negative trigger
Optionally reinforce
Negative triggers are most effective in the description itself
Argument format / valid values
Yes — JSON Schema
No
JSON Schema enum/pattern/minimum are the canonical way to constrain args
Global agent persona / goals
No
Yes
System prompt is for cross-cutting instructions; tool desc is tool-specific
Tool prioritisation (prefer A over B)
No
Yes
Relative priority is a global concern, not local to either tool description
Additional Code Example: Multi-Tool System with Scope Guards
Advanced: Describing Parameters with JSON Schema Constraints
The
input_schema
field accepts full JSON Schema. Beyond basic types, you can express enums, patterns, ranges, and descriptions per-parameter. Each constraint reduces the chance of the model passing invalid arguments:
Real-World Example: Customer Support Agent Tool Suite

A customer support agent has access to six tools. Poor descriptions cause incorrect tool selection — e.g., using "lookup_order" when the user asks about a product. Here is the complete, production-quality tool suite with precise descriptions:
Common Mistakes
Mistake
Effect
Fix
Description only says WHAT the tool does, not WHEN to call it
Model calls the tool on tangentially related queries; too many spurious calls
Add an explicit "Use when..." sentence and a "Do NOT call if..." sentence
Ambiguous tool name (e.g., get_data, run, process)
Model cannot distinguish it from other tools; unpredictable selection
Use verb_noun format that states intent: get_order_status, send_confirmation_email
No enum on constrained string parameters
Model passes "Electronics" or "tech" instead of "electronics"; dispatch fails
Add enum:["electronics","clothing",...] to the JSON Schema property
Missing description on parameters
Model guesses argument format; wrong UUID format, wrong date format, etc.
Every parameter must have a description with format example and constraints
Not distinguishing two tools with overlapping domains
Model randomly picks either tool for the same query
Explicitly contrast tools: "Use search_faq for general policy questions; use get_order_status for specific orders"
Describing side effects insufficiently (e.g., send_email without "only after confirmation")
Model triggers destructive or irreversible action speculatively
Always note destructive/irreversible side effects and add explicit confirmation guard in description
Best Practices
Use the five-question template for every tool description:
(1) What does it return? (2) When should the model call it? (3) When should it NOT call it? (4) What are argument constraints? (5) What side effects does it have?
Use verb_noun naming.
get_order_status, send_confirmation_email, search_knowledge_base — the name itself reduces ambiguity before the description is even read.
Add enums for all constrained string parameters.
If an argument has a fixed set of valid values, enumerate them. The model will choose from the enum rather than inventing values.
Explicitly contrast overlapping tools.
If two tools cover related domains, add a sentence to each description distinguishing when to use one vs the other.
Test with adversarial queries.
After defining tools, test with 10 queries that should NOT trigger the tool. If the model calls it anyway, tighten the negative trigger in the description.
Keep descriptions under 200 words.
Verbose descriptions consume tokens and risk diluting the critical trigger sentences. Every word should earn its place.
Version tool descriptions with the model version.
Different Claude versions respond differently to the same description. Test after model upgrades.
Performance and Scalability

Tool definitions are included verbatim in every API request. With 10 tools averaging 150 words each (≈200 tokens), every call carries a constant 2,000-token overhead for the tool catalogue. At a volume of 100,000 calls/day, this is 200 million overhead tokens per day. Strategies to reduce this:
Contextual tool filtering.
Include only tools relevant to the current task context. A billing query does not need shipping tools. Reduces per-call tool tokens by 60-80%.
Prompt caching.
Anthropic's prompt caching API caches the system + tools block across calls with the same tool definitions. Cache hits cost 10% of the original input token price.
Tool description compression.
Shorten descriptions to the minimum that preserves correct selection behaviour. Remove examples if the enum makes them redundant.

"""
Contextual tool selection: include only relevant tools per request type.
"""
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

ALL_TOOLS = {
    "order": [{"name": "get_order_status", "description": "...", "input_schema": {"type":"object","properties":{}}}],
    "billing": [{"name": "get_invoice", "description": "...", "input_schema": {"type":"object","properties":{}}}],
    "faq": [{"name": "search_faq", "description": "...", "input_schema": {"type":"object","properties":{}}}],
}

INTENT_KEYWORDS = {
    "order": ["order", "shipping", "delivery", "tracking", "package"],
    "billing": ["invoice", "payment", "charge", "refund", "billing"],
    "faq": ["policy", "how", "what", "can i", "help"],
}

def select_tools(user_message: str) -> list:
    msg_lower = user_message.lower()
    selected = []
    for category, keywords in INTENT_KEYWORDS.items():
        if any(kw in msg_lower for kw in keywords):
            selected.extend(ALL_TOOLS[category])
    return selected or ALL_TOOLS["faq"]  # fallback to FAQ

# Only order tools for order query — saves 2 tool definitions (~400 tokens)
tools = select_tools("Where is my order ORD-ABC12345?")
print(f"Selected {len(tools)} tool(s): {[t['name'] for t in tools]}")
# Selected 1 tool(s): ['get_order_status']
Tooling and Ecosystem
Approach
Tool definition format
Notes
Anthropic Claude API
tools=[{name, description, input_schema}]
input_schema is full JSON Schema draft-07; stop_reason=tool_use signals invocation
OpenAI function calling
tools=[{type:'function', function:{name, description, parameters}}]
Similar structure; parameters is JSON Schema
Model Context Protocol (MCP)
Tools declared in MCP server manifest
Structured discovery protocol; tool descriptions served via MCP server at runtime
LangChain
@tool
decorator or Tool class
Description from docstring or args_schema; serialised to provider format automatically
LlamaIndex
FunctionTool / QueryEngineTool
Tool description passed as fn_schema or description parameter
Pydantic AI
@agent.tool decorator with typed function signatures
Auto-generates JSON Schema from Python type hints; description from docstring
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
How model knows about tool
Argument validation
Best for
Tool descriptions (this topic)
Reads description and JSON Schema in context
JSON Schema enum/pattern/range
Structured tool calls with type-safe arguments
System prompt instructions
Free-form instruction in system prompt: "when user asks X, call function Y"
None (model free-generates args)
Simple one-tool agents; prototyping
ReAct text parsing
Model emits Action: tool[arg] as plain text; loop parses
Regex on text; fragile
Models that pre-date native tool_use API support
MCP server discovery
Tool list served dynamically at runtime by MCP server
JSON Schema via MCP manifest
Multi-tool systems where available tools change per session
Constrained generation (grammar)
Model output constrained to a JSON grammar; no description needed
Grammar-level; very strict
Structured extraction with fixed schema; not dynamic tool selection
Security and Reliability

Tool descriptions define what the model believes a tool does. An attacker who can modify tool descriptions (e.g., via a malicious MCP server or prompt injection in a dynamically generated description) can redirect the model's tool calls. Never construct tool descriptions from user-controlled input.

Tools with destructive side effects (deleting records, sending emails, charging payments) require explicit scope guards in their descriptions AND confirmation logic in the dispatcher. The description is a prompt — it can be overridden by a sufficiently adversarial user message. Defence in depth means the dispatcher enforces its own access controls independent of the model's tool selection decision.
Never generate tool descriptions from user input.
Descriptions are privileged instructions; treat them like code, not user content.
Enforce access control in the dispatcher, not just the description.
The description tells the model when to call; the dispatcher enforces whether it is allowed.
Audit all tool invocations.
Log tool_use blocks with caller identity, arguments, and result for security review and anomaly detection.
Rate-limit destructive tools.
A maximum of N calls to send_email or initiate_refund per session prevents a compromised agent from bulk-invoking destructive actions.
Validate tool arguments server-side.
JSON Schema constraints in the tool definition guide the model but do not enforce validity. Re-validate all arguments in your dispatcher before executing.
Key Takeaways
The description field does most of the work.
It is the model's sole source of knowledge about when to call a tool, what it returns, and what its limits are.
Good descriptions answer five questions:
what it returns, when to call it (positive trigger), when NOT to call it (negative trigger), argument constraints, and side effects.
Use JSON Schema enums for constrained parameters.
Enums prevent the model from inventing invalid argument values and are the most reliable way to constrain string arguments.
Negative triggers are as important as positive triggers.
Explicitly stating what the tool does NOT do prevents spurious calls on edge cases.
Test descriptions adversarially.
Send queries that should NOT trigger the tool. If the model calls it anyway, add stronger negative triggers.
Tool descriptions consume tokens.
Use contextual tool filtering to include only relevant tools per request, and leverage prompt caching to reduce repeated tool catalogue costs.
Security requires defence in depth:
descriptions guide the model, but the dispatcher must enforce its own access controls and argument validation independent of what the model decides.
